blocks.text: The patient is a 77 year old female who presents for a lower extremity evaluation. The condition involves the right lower extremity.
Transition into care is described as the following:
The patient is transitioning into care from another physician and a summary of care was not provided.
Patient presents to clinic today with problems related to his right leg. He has chronic lymphocytic leukemia which has been
known for several years. He had an episode where he bumped his leg recently and had onset of pain. A fracture was
diagnosed and it was found to be within the lesion. Subsequent imaging has been completed and he wishes to be evaluated
for the etiology and treatment options for the lesion. He has an aching sensation in the leg with weightbearing. He denies
weight loss. He is at his baseline level of health otherwise.
Head, ears, eyes, nose, and throat: Atraumatic and normocephalic.
Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Skin: No open lesions, rashes or wounds. No café-au-lait spots. No dark or hairy patches present.
Vascular: Normal distal pulses all limbs. Capillary refill less than 2 seconds all limbs. No adenopathy present. No stasis
changes about ankles. Normal finger and toenails.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II - XII grossly intact. Light touch intact all limbs. Motor exam is 5/5 upper and lower extremities.
Knee: Limb length symmetric. Patella tracking in the mid line. No patella apprehension sign. No effusion present. No
crepitus with motion. No joint line tenderness. 1A Lockman's exam bilaterally. No McMurray's either knee. Range of motion
Right leg examination shows that he is moderately tender over the lateral aspect of the leg with a palpable mass in the mid
lateral aspect. There is no break in the skin. There is no discoloration. No venous dilatation. He can flex and extend the
knee without difficulty. He can flex extend the ankle without difficulty.
Plain film radiographs of the tibia and fibula on the right side are reviewed. They show an infiltrative destructive lesion in the
mid diaphysis with a transverse fracture that has some bridging callus visible.
MRI of the leg is reviewed and it shows an expansile destructive lesion with soft tissue extension involving the mid aspect of
Bone scan is reviewed and it was equivocal, moderate increase in activity in the right lower extremity.
2. Right fibula infiltrative destructive lesion concerning for sarcoma.
The patient is 77 years old and we discussed the fact that I think he has a sarcoma and that chemotherapy is not a good
option at his age due to the neurologic complications that can occur.
In light of these facts I am recommending radical excision of the mid aspect of the fibula for diagnostic and therapeutic
purposes. Once tissue diagnosis has been obtained then we can decide whether radiation therapy will be necessary for local
We discussed the general behavior of sarcomas. We discussed the fact that the differential diagnosis would be an infection
but this has a very aggressive appearance for an infection with no history of prior trauma direct inoculation.
In anticipation of surgery he is given a letter requesting cardiac risk stratification.
A total of 50 minutes was spent in direct contact with the patient interviewing the patient, examining the patient, reviewing
imaging studies with the patient, reviewing reports with the patient and formulating this treatment plan.
Reviewed HPI, History and ROS at today's visit.
The dictated information, contained herein, was transcribed with voice recognition software. Although reviewed, the content
lidocaine HCl-hydrocortison ac (3-0.5% cream, topical) Active.

Neurological Present- Tingling. Not Present- Burning, Decreased Memory, Difficulty Using Hands, Numbness, Seizures, Stroke and

Pathological fracture of fibula, initial encounter ( M84.469A)
Malignant neoplasm of right fibula ( C40.21)
Pre-op studies to be completed by PCP
